Applied Machine Learning: In-class Exercise 05-1¶

Goal¶

Our goal for this exercise sheet is to learn the basics of supervised learning using scikit-learn, assess if a model is well-calibrated, and calibrate it using calibration methods. We will do this using a train-validate-test split: the model is first fitted on training data and then calibrated on validation data. Test data will be used to compare the performance of the uncalibrated and calibrated models. Later, we will explore how to construct more refined resampling workflows for calibration.

Required packages¶

We will use scikit-learn for machine learning:

In [1]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

rng = np.random.default_rng(123)

# Unlike R, we cannot load the `cells` dataset from the library. 
# So we provide the data along with the exercise sheet.
# @matthias: This is the most convenient way, and this csv file is < 2MB. 
# So it is fine to include it in the repo.
cells = pd.read_csv("./cells.csv")

# Remove the unwanted 'case' column if it exists.
if 'case' in cells.columns:
    cells = cells.drop(columns=['case'])

1 Checking cardinality properties¶

1.1 Creating a split with scikit-learn¶

Let’s use the train_test_split() function from scikit-learn to create a train-test-validation split. We will split the data twice: First, create a train-test split, and then split the training data again into training and validation datasets.

Perform the splits as follows:

First, create a train-test split with 60% of the data assigned to training and validation, and 40% to testing. Use stratification based on the class column to preserve class proportions. Next, split the resulting training set into training (70%) and validation (30%) datasets, again stratified by the class variable. Finally, separate each resulting dataset into predictors (X) and the outcome variable (y).

Check the shapes of the datasets to confirm the splits:

In [2]:
#===SOLUTION===

cells_train_val, cells_test = train_test_split(
    cells,
    train_size=0.6,
    stratify=cells['class'], 
    random_state=123
)

cells_train, cells_val = train_test_split(
    cells_train_val,
    train_size=0.7,
    stratify=cells_train_val['class'],
    random_state=123
)

X_train = cells_train.drop(columns=['class'])
y_train = cells_train['class']

X_val = cells_val.drop(columns=['class'])
y_val = cells_val['class']

X_test = cells_test.drop(columns=['class'])
y_test = cells_test['class']

print(f"Training set shape: X: {X_train.shape}, y: {y_train.shape}")
print(f"Validation set shape: X: {X_val.shape}, y: {y_val.shape}")
print(f"Test set shape: X: {X_test.shape}, y: {y_test.shape}")

Training set shape: X: (847, 56), y: (847,)
Validation set shape: X: (364, 56), y: (364,)
Test set shape: X: (808, 56), y: (808,)

1.2 Training a Naive Bayes model¶

We’ll illustrate the utility of calibration tools by using a type of model that, in this instance, is likely to produce a poorly calibrated outcome. The Naive Bayes classifier is a well-established algorithm that assumes predictors are statistically independent (to simplify computations). While this assumption does not hold true for our data, Naive Bayes can still effectively discriminate between classes. Unfortunately, when many predictors are included, this classifier tends to produce poorly calibrated probability distributions. Predicted probabilities frequently gravitate toward extremes (close to 0 or 1), resulting in a characteristic "U"-shaped distribution (Kuhn and Johnson, 2013).

To demonstrate, set up and train a Naive Bayes classifier using sklearn.naive_bayes.GaussianNB class. Use your training dataset (X_train and y_train) to fit the model.

In [3]:
###===SOLUTION===

from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB

nb_model = GaussianNB()
nb_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[3]:
GaussianNB()
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
GaussianNB()

1.3 Predicting on unseen test data¶

Next, use the trained model to predict probabilities on unseen test data. In scikit-learn, calling .predict_proba() on the Naive Bayes model will return predicted probabilities for each class. Since this is a binary classification problem, you only need the predicted probabilities for class "PS" to evaluate model performance in the next step.

Perform the following steps:

Predict class probabilities using .predict_proba(). Identify the column corresponding to class "PS". Predict the class labels using .predict(). Create a DataFrame containing the actual class labels, predicted class labels, and the predicted probabilities for class "PS". Print out the head.

In [4]:
#===SOLUTION===

# Get predicted probabilities for each class.
pred_probs = nb_model.predict_proba(X_test)

# Determine the index for class "PS".
ps_index = list(nb_model.classes_).index("PS")

# Get predicted classes
pred_class = nb_model.predict(X_test)

test_df_nb = pd.DataFrame({
    "class": y_test,
    "pred_class": pred_class,
    "pred_prob": pred_probs[:, ps_index]
})

test_df_nb.head()
Out[4]:
class pred_class pred_prob
400 PS PS 9.999873e-01
1389 WS PS 8.673478e-01
1971 WS WS 1.550348e-03
81 PS PS 9.982106e-01
604 PS WS 8.049752e-13

1.4 Assessing model performance¶

To evaluate how well the model performs, we will use two commonly used metrics from scikit-learn: ROC AUC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve) measures the model’s ability to correctly discriminate between the two classes.

Brier Score measures the accuracy of the predicted probabilities — that is, how close the predicted probabilities are to the actual outcomes. It reflects how well-calibrated the model is in expressing confidence in its predictions.

To compute these metrics:

  1. Convert the true labels to binary values: use 1 for the positive class "PS" and 0 for "WS".
  2. Use sklearn.metrics.roc_auc_score() to compute the ROC AUC based on the predicted probabilities for class "PS".
  3. Use sklearn.metrics.brier_score_loss() to compute the Brier score.
In [5]:
#===SOLUTION===

from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score, brier_score_loss

# Map true labels to binary: 1 for "PS" (positive class) and 0 for "WS"
y_test_binary = (test_df_nb["class"] == "PS").astype(int)

# Compute ROC AUC using the predicted probability for class "PS"
roc_auc = roc_auc_score(y_test_binary, test_df_nb["pred_prob"])
print(f"ROC AUC: {roc_auc:.3f}")

# Compute the Brier score (Brier score loss)
brier = brier_score_loss(y_test_binary, test_df_nb["pred_prob"])
print(f"Brier Score: {brier:.3f}")

ROC AUC: 0.842
Brier Score: 0.188

1.5 Assessing model calibration¶

We provide the following function to simplify the visualization of calibration curves and to compute the Expected Calibration Error (ECE), a commonly used calibration metric. These plots divide the predicted probabilities into equally sized bins and compare the average predicted probability within each bin to the actual observed event rate. For example, if a bin captures predictions between 20% and 30%, a well-calibrated model would have roughly 25% of the samples in that bin belonging to the positive class. We will create calibration plots using both 10 and 5 bins to visually assess the model’s calibration. The objective is to evaluate how closely the predicted probabilities match the true class frequencies across bins. In our implementation, we rely on sklearn.calibration.calibration_curve and wrap it in a custom function to streamline the process.

In [6]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve


def plot_calibration_curves(pred_df, y_binary, title_prefix="Uncalibrated"):
    """
    Plot histogram of predicted probabilities and calibration curves.
    
    Parameters:
    -----------
    pred_df : pandas.DataFrame
        DataFrame containing the predicted probabilities in column 'pred_prob'
    y_binary : array-like
        Binary target values (1 for positive class, 0 for negative class)
    title_prefix : str, default="Uncalibrated"
        Prefix for plot titles
    """
    # Create a figure with 3 subplots
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))
    
    # Plot histogram of predicted probabilities for "PS" in the first subplot
    axes[0].hist(pred_df["pred_prob"], bins=np.linspace(0, 1, 11), edgecolor='black')
    axes[0].set_xlabel("Predicted probability for 'PS'")
    axes[0].set_ylabel("Frequency")
    axes[0].set_title("Histogram of predicted probabilities")

    # Compute calibration curve data using 10 bins (uniformly spaced)
    frac_pos_10, mean_pred_10 = calibration_curve(y_binary, pred_df["pred_prob"], n_bins=10, strategy='uniform')

    # Compute calibration curve data using 5 bins (uniformly spaced)
    frac_pos_5, mean_pred_5 = calibration_curve(y_binary, pred_df["pred_prob"], n_bins=5, strategy='uniform')
    
    # Compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE)
    # For 10 bins
    ece_10 = 0
    bin_counts_10 = np.histogram(pred_df["pred_prob"], bins=10, range=(0, 1))[0]
    for i in range(len(frac_pos_10)):
        if bin_counts_10[i] > 0:  # Avoid empty bins
            ece_10 += (bin_counts_10[i] / len(y_binary)) * abs(frac_pos_10[i] - mean_pred_10[i])
    
    # For 5 bins
    ece_5 = 0
    bin_counts_5 = np.histogram(pred_df["pred_prob"], bins=5, range=(0, 1))[0]
    for i in range(len(frac_pos_5)):
        if bin_counts_5[i] > 0:  # Avoid empty bins
            ece_5 += (bin_counts_5[i] / len(y_binary)) * abs(frac_pos_5[i] - mean_pred_5[i])

    # Calibration plot for 10 bins in the second subplot
    axes[1].plot(mean_pred_10, frac_pos_10, marker='o', linestyle='-', label='Calibration')
    axes[1].plot([0, 1], [0, 1], linestyle='--', color='gray')  # reference line for perfect calibration
    axes[1].set_title(f"{title_prefix} - 10 bins; ECE: {ece_10:.3f}")
    axes[1].set_xlabel("Mean predicted probability")
    axes[1].set_ylabel("Fraction of positives")
    axes[1].set_xlim([0, 1])
    axes[1].set_ylim([0, 1])

    # Calibration plot for 5 bins in the third subplot
    axes[2].plot(mean_pred_5, frac_pos_5, marker='o', linestyle='-', label='Calibration')
    axes[2].plot([0, 1], [0, 1], linestyle='--', color='gray')
    axes[2].set_title(f"{title_prefix} - 5 bins; ECE: {ece_5:.3f}")
    axes[2].set_xlabel("Mean predicted probability")
    axes[2].set_ylabel("Fraction of positives")
    axes[2].set_xlim([0, 1])
    axes[2].set_ylim([0, 1])

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Task: Call the function with the current predictions on the test set.

In [7]:
#===SOLUTION===

plot_calibration_curves(test_df_nb, y_test_binary)

2 Platt Scaling¶

The good news is that we can improve the probability estimates. Several techniques exist to adjust the predicted probabilities so they better reflect the actual observed outcomes — in other words, to improve calibration. Different methods correct these estimates in different ways.

One of the most common approaches is Platt Scaling, also called logistic calibration. The process works as follows: the original model is used to make probability predictions on a validation dataset. These predicted probabilities, which are often poorly calibrated, are then used as input to a logistic regression model. The logistic regression learns to correct the systematic errors in the original model’s probabilities. Conceptually, this creates a stacked model:

Original Model → Probabilities → Logistic Regression → Calibrated Probabilities.

If successful, the logistic regression identifies regions where the original model tends to under- or over-predict. For example, if the model typically predicts around 2% probability for certain samples, but the observed frequency is actually 7%, the logistic model will learn to adjust those predictions upwards accordingly.

2.1 Platt Scaling with scikit-learn¶

2.1.1 Task¶

First, we need to compute the predicted probabilities of the Naive Bayes model on the validation data:

  • Get the predicted probabilities on the validation set.
  • Extract the predicted probabilities specifically for the "PS" class.
  • Convert the true validation labels into a binary format (1 for "PS", 0 for "WS").

Next, fit a logistic regression model where:

  • The input X is the predicted probability from the uncalibrated Naive Bayes model.
  • The output y is the true label (in binary form) from the validation data.

This logistic regression model will serve as the calibrator, adjusting the Naive Bayes probabilities to become better calibrated.

In [8]:
#===SOLUTION===

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# Get predicted probabilities on the validation set using the uncalibrated model.
# The output is an array with one probability per class.
pred_probs_val = nb_model.predict_proba(X_val)

# Determine the index corresponding to "PS"
ps_index = list(nb_model.classes_).index("PS")

# Extract the predicted probability for class "PS"
pred_prob_val = pred_probs_val[:, ps_index]

# Convert the true validation labels to binary: 1 if "PS", 0 if "WS"
y_val_binary = (y_val == "PS").astype(int)

# Reshape the predicted probabilities to a 2D array (required by scikit-learn)
X_calib = pred_prob_val.reshape(-1, 1)

# Fit a logistic regression model for Platt scaling (i.e., logistic calibration)
calibrator_logreg = LogisticRegression(solver='lbfgs', penalty='l2').fit(X_calib, y_val_binary)

# Output the fitted logistic regression parameters
print(f"Platt scaling model fitted:")
print(f"  Intercept: {calibrator_logreg.intercept_[0]:.3f}")
print(f"  Coefficient for predicted probability: {calibrator_logreg.coef_[0][0]:.3f}")

Platt scaling model fitted:
  Intercept: -0.661
  Coefficient for predicted probability: 2.255

2.1.2 Discussion¶

How do we know if the calibration actually worked? Since we used the training data to fit the Naive Bayes model and the validation data to fit the logistic regression (Platt scaling) model, we now need to assess the performance of the combined model on the test data.

To do this:

  • First, obtain the predicted probabilities from the original (uncalibrated) Naive Bayes model on the test set.
  • Then, pass these probabilities through the logistic regression calibrator to obtain calibrated probabilities.
  • After obtaining the calibrated probabilities, create a DataFrame that contains:
    • The true class labels.
    • The predicted class labels based on a threshold (e.g., 0.5).
    • The calibrated predicted probabilities.

Finally, assess the calibration and performance of the calibrated model by:

  1. Calculating the ROC AUC and Brier score of the calibrated model.
  2. Plotting the calibration curve and comparing it to the curve from the uncalibrated model, using our implemented plotting function. Plot the curve for the uncalibrated NB model, and the plot the curve for the calibrated one, so that you can directly compare them.

This will help you determine whether the calibration improved the model's reliability.

In [9]:
#===SOLUTION===


# Get predicted probabilities on the test set using the uncalibrated model
pred_probs_test = nb_model.predict_proba(X_test)
ps_index = list(nb_model.classes_).index("PS")
pred_prob_test = pred_probs_test[:, ps_index]

# Apply the calibrator to the test set predictions
X_test_calib = pred_prob_test.reshape(-1, 1)
calibrated_probs = calibrator_logreg.predict_proba(X_test_calib)[:, 1]

# Create a DataFrame with the calibrated predictions
test_df_platt = pd.DataFrame({
    'class': y_test,
    'pred_class': np.where(calibrated_probs >= 0.5, "PS", "WS"),
    'pred_prob': calibrated_probs
})

# Convert true labels to binary: 1 for "PS", 0 for "WS"
y_test_binary = (y_test == "PS").astype(int)

# Compute ROC AUC and Brier score for the calibrated model
roc_auc_calibrated = roc_auc_score(y_test_binary, test_df_platt["pred_prob"])
brier_calibrated = brier_score_loss(y_test_binary, test_df_platt["pred_prob"])

print(f"Calibrated model ROC AUC: {roc_auc_calibrated:.3f}")
print(f"Calibrated model Brier Score: {brier_calibrated:.3f}")

# Plot calibration curves for both uncalibrated and calibrated models
plot_calibration_curves(test_df_nb, y_test_binary, title_prefix="Uncalibrated Naive Bayes")
plot_calibration_curves(test_df_platt, y_test_binary, title_prefix="Platt-Calibrated Naive Bayes")

Calibrated model ROC AUC: 0.842
Calibrated model Brier Score: 0.162

Discuss your observations from the ROC AUC, Brier Score and the calibration curves.

===SOLUTION===

We observe a modest improvement after calibration. The Brier score, evaluated on the test data, has improved, indicating that the calibrated probabilities are now closer to the true outcomes. Interestingly, the ROC AUC remains unchanged. This is expected, as the ROC AUC measures the model's ability to discriminate between classes. Since Platt scaling only adjusts the predicted probabilities (without altering the ranking of predictions), it typically has little to no effect on the ROC AUC. The calibration curves also show noticeable improvement.

The curves of the calibrated model align more closely with the diagonal, indicating better agreement between predicted probabilities and observed event rates. Additionally, the ECE has decreased, further confirming that the calibration procedure improved the reliability of the model's probability estimates.

3 Isotonic regression¶

A different approach to calibration is to use isotonic regression. In a manner similar to logistic calibration, use probably to calibrate the Naive Bayes model using isotonic regression. Do we improve the calibration in comparison to Platt scaling?

Use sklearn.isotonic.IsotonicRegression to calibrate the predictions. Compute the ROC AUC and Brier Score. In addition, plot the calibration curve for the uncalibrated model, Platt scaling-calibrated model, Isotonic regression-calibrated model. Disscuss your observations.

In [10]:
#===SOLUTION===

from sklearn.isotonic import IsotonicRegression

# -----------------------------
# 1. Fit the isotonic regression calibrator using validation set predictions
# -----------------------------
calibrator_iso = IsotonicRegression(out_of_bounds='clip')
calibrator_iso.fit(pred_prob_val, y_val_binary)

# -----------------------------
# 2. Apply the isotonic regression calibrator to the test set predictions
# -----------------------------
# Apply the fitted isotonic regression model to adjust the uncalibrated test probabilities
X_test_calib = test_df_nb["pred_prob"].values.reshape(-1, 1)
iso_pred_prob = calibrator_iso.predict(test_df_nb["pred_prob"].values)

# Create a new DataFrame for the isotonic calibrated test predictions
test_df_iso = test_df_nb.copy()
test_df_iso["pred_prob"] = iso_pred_prob
test_df_iso["pred_class"] = np.where(iso_pred_prob >= 0.5, "PS", "WS")

# -----------------------------
# 3. Evaluate performance of the isotonic calibrated model
# -----------------------------
# Convert true labels to binary: 1 for "PS", 0 for "WS"
y_test_binary = (test_df_iso["class"] == "PS").astype(int)

roc_auc_iso = roc_auc_score(y_test_binary, test_df_iso["pred_prob"])
brier_iso = brier_score_loss(y_test_binary, test_df_iso["pred_prob"])

print(f"Isotonic calibrated ROC AUC: {roc_auc_iso:.3f}")
print(f"Isotonic calibrated Brier Score: {brier_iso:.3f}")

# -----------------------------
# 4. Plot calibration curves using the existing function
# -----------------------------
# Plot calibration curves for both uncalibrated and calibrated models
plot_calibration_curves(test_df_nb, y_test_binary, title_prefix="Uncalibrated Naive Bayes")
plot_calibration_curves(test_df_platt, y_test_binary, title_prefix="Platt-Calibrated Naive Bayes")
plot_calibration_curves(test_df_iso, y_test_binary, title_prefix="Isotonic-Calibrated Naive Bayes")

Isotonic calibrated ROC AUC: 0.836
Isotonic calibrated Brier Score: 0.158

===SOLUTION===

It seems that Isotonic Regression can further improve the performance compared to Platt Scaling. Note that this observation is different from the R version.

4 Resampling for Calibration¶

So far, we've used a simple train-validation-test split to calibrate and evaluate our models. However, in practice, we often prefer using resampling techniques, such as cross-validation, to more robustly estimate model calibration performance.

In this section, we apply 10-fold cross-validation to fit and calibrate Naive Bayes models. Rather than using Platt scaling, we will demonstrate an alternative method: beta calibration. This is a more flexible, non-linear calibration technique based on a logistic-like transformation of predicted probabilities.

scikit-learn does not provide function of beta calibration. Here, we provide an implementation of beta calibration following Algorithm 1 from Kull et al. (2017).

In [11]:
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold
from scipy.optimize import minimize


# Implementation of Algorithm 1 from https://proceedings.mlr.press/v54/kull17a.html

def beta_calibration_loss(params, p, y):
    """Negative log-likelihood for beta calibration.
    
    Parameters:
      params: array-like, [a, c]
      p: uncalibrated probabilities (predicted for positive class)
      y: true binary labels (1 for "PS", 0 for "WS")
    """
    a, c = params
    # Avoid log(0) issues by clipping
    p = np.clip(p, 1e-15, 1 - 1e-15)
    log_term = a * np.log(p / (1 - p)) + c 
    q = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-log_term))  # Corrected sign
    loss = -np.sum(y * np.log(q) + (1 - y) * np.log(1 - q))
    return loss

def fit_beta_calibration(p, y):
    """Fit beta calibration parameters.
    
    Returns optimal parameters (a, c) estimated via minimization.
    """
    initial_guess = np.array([1.0, 0.0]) 
    result = minimize(beta_calibration_loss, initial_guess, args=(p, y), method='L-BFGS-B')
    return result.x 

def beta_calibration_predict(p, params):
    """Apply beta calibration using parameters from fit_beta_calibration.
    
    Returns calibrated probabilities.
    """
    a, c = params
    p = np.clip(p, 1e-15, 1 - 1e-15)
    log_term = a * np.log(p / (1 - p)) + c 
    q = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-log_term))  
    return q

You can conduct the CV with beta calibration as follows.

For each CV fold:

  1. Train the base model: Fit a Naive Bayes classifier on the training fold using nb_model.fit().
  2. Predict uncalibrated probabilities: Use nb_model.predict_proba() on the validation fold to obtain uncalibrated probabilities. Then, extract the probabilities for the "PS" class.
  3. Fit the beta calibration model: Call fit_beta_calibration(predictions, true_labels) to estimate the calibration parameters (a and c in Algorithm 1 of Kull et al. 2017) based on the uncalibrated probabilities and the true labels of the validation fold.
  4. Apply beta calibration: Use beta_calibration_predict(predictions, params) to obtain calibrated probabilities for the validation fold.
  5. Evaluate both models: Compute the ROC AUC and Brier Score for both the uncalibrated and beta-calibrated probabilities.

After completing all folds, aggregate the results to compare the average ROC AUC, brier score. You can also visualize the calibration curves.

In [12]:
#===SOLUTION===

# 10-fold CV with beta calibration
skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=10, shuffle=True, random_state=123)

uncalibrated_roc_auc = []
uncalibrated_brier = []
beta_roc_auc = []
beta_brier = []
all_true = []
all_uncal_pred = []
all_beta_pred = []

for train_idx, val_idx in skf.split(cells, cells['class']):
    train_fold = cells.iloc[train_idx]
    val_fold = cells.iloc[val_idx]
    
    X_train_fold = train_fold.drop(columns=['class'])
    y_train_fold = train_fold['class']
    X_val_fold = val_fold.drop(columns=['class'])
    y_val_fold = val_fold['class']
    
    # Fit Naive Bayes on training fold.
    nb_model = GaussianNB()
    nb_model.fit(X_train_fold, y_train_fold)
    
    # Predict probabilities on the validation fold.
    prob_val = nb_model.predict_proba(X_val_fold)
    ps_index = list(nb_model.classes_).index("PS")
    uncal_pred = prob_val[:, ps_index]
    
    # Convert true labels to binary: 1 for "PS", 0 for "WS"
    y_val_bin = (y_val_fold == "PS").astype(int).values
    
    # Fit beta calibration using our custom function.
    params = fit_beta_calibration(uncal_pred, y_val_bin)
    beta_pred = beta_calibration_predict(uncal_pred, params)
    
    # Compute metrics for uncalibrated predictions.
    roc_uncal = roc_auc_score(y_val_bin, uncal_pred)
    brier_uncal = brier_score_loss(y_val_bin, uncal_pred)
    # And for beta calibrated predictions.
    roc_beta = roc_auc_score(y_val_bin, beta_pred)
    brier_beta = brier_score_loss(y_val_bin, beta_pred)
    
    uncalibrated_roc_auc.append(roc_uncal)
    uncalibrated_brier.append(brier_uncal)
    beta_roc_auc.append(roc_beta)
    beta_brier.append(brier_beta)
    
    # Store for calibration plot.
    all_true.extend(y_val_bin)
    all_uncal_pred.extend(uncal_pred)
    all_beta_pred.extend(beta_pred)

print(f"Uncalibrated model: Average ROC AUC: {np.mean(uncalibrated_roc_auc):.3f}; Brier Score: {np.mean(uncalibrated_brier):.3f}")
print(f"Beta calibrated model: Average ROC AUC: {np.mean(beta_roc_auc):.3f}; Brier Score: {np.mean(beta_brier):.3f}")

uncal_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'class': ['PS' if y == 1 else 'WS' for y in all_true],
    'pred_prob': all_uncal_pred,
    'pred_class': ['PS' if p >= 0.5 else 'WS' for p in all_uncal_pred]
})

beta_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'class': ['PS' if y == 1 else 'WS' for y in all_true],
    'pred_prob': all_beta_pred,
    'pred_class': ['PS' if p >= 0.5 else 'WS' for p in all_beta_pred]
})

# Convert true labels to binary for plotting
y_true_binary = np.array(all_true)

# Plot calibration curves using the existing function
plot_calibration_curves(uncal_df, y_true_binary, title_prefix="Uncalibrated (CV)")
plot_calibration_curves(beta_df, y_true_binary, title_prefix="Beta-Calibrated (CV)")

Uncalibrated model: Average ROC AUC: 0.808; Brier Score: 0.209
Beta calibrated model: Average ROC AUC: 0.808; Brier Score: 0.204

Summary¶

In this exercise sheet, we learned how to fit a classification model using scikit-learn and how to assess its performance on unseen test data. We demonstrated how to manually split the data into training, validation, and test sets, and how to assess model calibration.

We explored how to diagnose poor calibration using calibration plots and metrics such as the Brier Score. We then applied Platt scaling (logistic calibration) and Isotonic Regression as techniques to improve the calibration of predicted probabilities.

It is also worth noting that in a real-world scenario, once the calibrator is fitted, we would typically retrain the Naive Bayes classifier on the combined training and validation data before applying it to the test data. This mirrors the logic of nested resampling and helps to reduce bias in performance estimation.

This tutorial is a Python adaptation of the original tidymodels introduction to calibration, used under a CC BY-SA license.
